there are postal companies which have a monopoly and are doing a reasonably good job.     2981.6167855777953
we need to protect this type of arrangement and denounce other things.     7173.4228040080525
mr president, commissioner, this morning on french national radio, i heard that liberalisation of the french postal service was now only a matter of days or hours away and that it was irreparable, like the developments with our neighbours in sweden, the netherlands and germany.     3066.7468892400284
this concerns us deeply and that is why we requested this debate today.     2137.8100569119115
the commission is committed to a universal postal service, as the european parliament has shown on several occasions, the postal service is actually, as some speakers have already mentioned, a key factor in social and territorial cohesion.     2717.2933379749784
in this connection i should like to ask one of our swedish meps for an opinion of the study which has been carried out and which shows that in fact in the most remote areas of sweden the postal service was rather poor in terms of distribution.     3427.518854311346
that is not the sort of postal service that we want.     3448.1453837808294
the economic value of the postal service was recognised by the treaty of amsterdam and we consider that it goes far beyond the distribution of mail.     4383.477366143392
it is the service which remains closest to the citizens and which we call a 'public service' , which makes it possible for everyone resident within european territory to receive the service, and this entitlement must remain an acquired right, an acquis communautaire.     6169.808815817048
the matter of the european union' s recognition of a high-quality postal service, available to all, remains valid today, and we are increasingly concerned.     6301.157996673258
that is why today we are asking not to have any nasty surprises in the committee on regional policy, transport and tourism come monday.     6027.740623805835
let us point out that parliament' s positions were clearly defined and that the group of the greens/european free alliance supported them.     10034.720433289245
this resolution of 14 january 1999 required that, prior to undertaking any liberalisation of this key sector, involving 1.7 million employees after all, the commission should provide impact studies for this liberalisation.     4503.741656185796
well, we are not convinced that the studies currently available make it possible to implement this liberalisation right now and we are concerned because it is well known that liberalisation without impact studies would cause thousands of job losses, something we certainly do not want.     3182.1422220854743
we value our public service, our universal postal service, because we know, and here too we are acting as representatives of the citizens, that the citizens value this service and the commission undertook, during mr bolkestein' s hearing, to maintain this universal service throughout the european union.     6540.1320338724045
i shall not repeat all the various statements he made at his hearing, i think we all still remember them.     4519.841826517511
we further maintain that we are waiting for this proper evaluation before any definitive decision, as stipulated in the directive and the revision of the 1997 directive.     3212.455631926575
we shall not accept a date for the liberalisation of all activities in the postal sector until the commission has offered us a guarantee that the universal service will be integrated into this directive and that the public postal service will be maintained in the states which consider this service essential to the citizen.     2508.0597790197216
mr president, i share the view that we need to make more progress from the point of view of efficiency and the analysis of the cost-effectiveness of the services offered.     2641.71779252247
but as an entrepreneur myself, i really cannot understand why it is always assumed this can only be done under the headings of "privatisation" and "liberalisation"!     5693.820565358777
or perhaps people are not concerned with creating the same conditions of access to the postal services for all, but are thinking only of the capital employment conditions for companies in large conglomerations.     1451.6394490910968
members should however represent all the citizens, which means it is their duty to think not in terms of business management but in terms of the national economy.     3079.1281504542017
in practice that means the following for the postal services: firstly, priority must be given to employment.     2594.6074382761512
i have nothing against liberalisation and privatisation, but the premises must be right.     4148.722666425638
secondly, we must guarantee that the citizens are not faced with reduced postal services.     5650.013139531426
examples in germany and sweden show that this certainly is a risk.     2691.7573926494965
mr stenmarck, sweden is the only country in which prices rose far higher after liberalisation than in all the other member states of the european union.     3920.673906360035
so that is another potential effect and one that we must prevent.     1127.1408743946906
thirdly, it is vital to have, first of all, a universal postal service at an affordable price for all citizens, wherever they may be; secondly, to have a uniform price for all member states; and thirdly, to have daily mail deliveries and collections on working days.     4167.768848087663
on the question of the cost-effectiveness of the various postage weights, it is worth considering further price cuts; but that is what the postal administration itself is saying.     2831.3478994694833
we do not have to adhere to a weight of over 300g when the analysis shows that we need to ensure a minimum weight of 150g to make it at all worthwhile.     4581.445263028799
in that respect i believe it was right and important to hold this debate today, to make mr bolkestein aware of parliament' s position and allow him to take certain aspects into account from the outset.     6489.292165299006
mr president, the 1997 directive, issued on 21 january 1998, which undertook the partial liberalisation of postal services in europe, envisaged a revision at a later date, in a rather convoluted manner, indeed, since it was to be dealt with in a commission proposal by the end of 1998, then decided by 1 january 2000, to come into force finally by 1 january 2003.
well, for the time being, the revision has not got off the starting block.     3453.742729623717
the two initial phases were not observed which, logically, ought to compromise the third.     3519.214446864886
my first question is this: surely the scheduled time limits were too short in any case?     2283.2912741795126
apparently, they were.     2583.130620335644
it is really not possible to see how the commission could by the end of 1998, as stipulated by article 7 of the directive, have evaluated the effects of a text which came into force in february of the same year.     2684.497353302317
moreover, the commission was also not able to provide the implementation report which, according to article 23, it was to submit by 31 december 2000.
should we complain about this delay?     8549.957906433348
we do not think so.     1510.1327137507458
if the commission actually did have convincing evidence in support of taking liberalisation forwards, no one doubts that it would present it without delay.     4256.352162196654
for its part, the european parliament, which had fought hard to ensure that the 1997 directive was balanced and respected the requirements of a universal public service, is rather satisfied to see that the compromise it obtained then is proving to be tenable.     2287.348283030914
in these circumstances, it is quite reasonable for this house to express concern today, asking an oral question which urges the commission to show the definite and unquestionable feasibility studies which to date no one has seen.     5664.819209579383
what are the scenarios for development and the potential risks?     1714.3742047622231
the main risk is that the commission, without adequate justification, might upset the balance which exists now by proposing to go swiftly on to a second phase of liberalisation which would, in some countries, throw off balance the funding of the universal postal service, making it unmanageable.     5087.942543522955
today, following the 1997 compromise, member states can give public monopolies responsibility for the postal service involving mail and parcels whose distribution cost is less than five times the basic tariff, with a weight of less than 350 grammes.     9120.103717777729
it may be supposed, for example, as commissioner bolkestein gave us to understand during his hearing on 6 september last year, that the commission might propose reducing these limits to 50 grams and two and a half times the basic tariff.     3148.4345163845114
this solution would not, in our opinion, be a fair one and indeed the commission would have no irrefutable figures to support it.     1284.3865590473492
but, unfortunately, we are aware since seattle that the commission has no need of a proper assessment of the previous stage before proposing that we go rapidly on to the next stage.     1822.987691342518
the second scenario, which we prefer: to defer the revision and maintain the current situation until such time as we do actually have a proper, detailed objective study of the effects of the 1997 directive.     4296.197722225789
in saying that, we are not seeking to protect monopolies whose pernicious effects we are already familiar with, but we wish to be certain that it is possible to combine the gradual evolution of the organisations with maintaining the key principles of public service.     4942.341758440723
finally, the union for a europe of nations group wonders if the idea of foisting unified management rules on the postal services of the fifteen member states might not be, essentially, flawed.     2774.957748523037
in france, for example, the unequal distribution of the population over a vast territorial area makes the obligation to provide a public service absolutely indispensable in mail distribution if one wishes to maintain the equality of citizens and social cohesion.     3642.9013587146
but other countries, in other situations, may have alternative analyses.     2660.3145410029665
in our opinion, each one must be free to make its own organisational choices.     5776.663017849966
in fact, article 86 of the ec treaty, formerly article 90, stipulates that services of general economic interest are subject to the rules on competition only insofar as the application of such rules does not obstruct the performance of the particular tasks assigned to them.     6549.387575658184
but who defines these tasks?     8976.938359131558
who established the limit beyond which this public service mission is compromised?     6727.582988550096
our answer is clear: it is not the commission nor even the council or the european parliament, but the people of each member state by democratic procedures.     1836.950549267996
to wish to proceed in any other way would be to distort the spirit of our institutions.     5184.1791003845465
mr president, we are currently in the first phase of transposition of directive 97/67/ce on postal services and i am terribly afraid that a new phase of liberalisation might be reflected in a significant reduction in the public service offered.     4357.057040086613
during mr bolkestein' s hearing, my colleague véronique mathieu asked for an undertaking "that any new proposals, once these are known, should be the subject of impact studies to determine the implications of this liberalisation programme before these provisions are adopted."     5585.515359740524
what has become of it?     1379.5689927522944
do we have the results of this impact study?     4167.892925637894
as you know, we members of the edd group, are in favour, along with others, of rural life, maintaining employment, and social cohesion in rural areas, a cohesion reinforced by high quality universal public service.     4788.186075033054
most assuredly i would like to be able to say that the examples i observe every day in my own country and in my own region, a very rural one, convince me that we can safely proceed to a new phase of liberalisation.     5131.559760809636
unfortunately, this is not the case and currently, by merging the start of the application of the european directive and the application of the 35-hour law, which is purely french, but also by changing the working time criteria, such as the 1 700 objects per hour to be handled per workstation as opposed to 1 400, as previously, in our rural areas we can observe either a reduction in public opening hours or a threat that some post offices will be closed.     2874.6608978089866
this is already an intolerable withdrawal of the public service.     2776.949013134292
i have here the local paper for my municipality, which reads, "the manager of the post office wishes to inform the local population that as of 3 january 2000" - you can see how topical this is - "the last collection will take place at 3.45 p.m. instead of at 4.30 p.m. as at present."     18515.20740816316
so you can appreciate why i am afraid of a phase of extensive liberalisation, and a package of measures which will entail a new wave of depopulation.     6817.931887292744
as you know, in our country, each village has its own local post office or at least a post box, with a collection every day.     5375.926860366949
the postman is not just a simple mail distribution operative but also the only social link with the local residents.     5696.996564381145
so he delivers money too, and sometimes even medicines, thus making it possible for isolated elderly people to be supported in living at home.     5114.956412720274
if postal services are liberalised in a poorly regulated way, more post offices will be closed, in the name of this infamous profitability, which is often the cause of rural regression.     5073.058427644564
once again, europe is distancing itself from its poorest and most vulnerable inhabitants.     3325.563858226748
they will be told that they are too expensive, without drawing any comparison with other social services such as police and health services but also sports and cultural services, where lack of profitability is scarcely admitted in urban areas.     5137.426976120431
everyone is free to choose their lifestyle.     3724.241039549759
they should be able to do so without compromising their equal opportunities and equal treatment.     8161.295995966247
in sport the saying goes, "do not change a winning team."     8101.165675216167
why should we wish to change a system which is satisfactory?     3126.0807856961374
the universal service will be able to fulfil its social mission only if it retains an area of monopoly enabling it to balance its budget.     9587.644047299664
we cannot afford to make a mistake, for we know for a fact that we shall not be able to turn the clock back and that in the event of an imbalance, the firm will know how to tackle it: jobs will be lost, services will be reduced, the dead wood will be removed and we already know which areas will be considered dead wood.     5022.056094629176
we are told we should change the limit from 350 grammes to 150 grammes, or even 50 grammes.     29854.578982219035
so be it.     611.2870776689049
but the 200 grammes that make up the difference will be seized upon by many private and therefore profitable bodies.     6972.246387374639
so, once again, why not leave things as they are and allow the public service to fund itself and to guarantee the annual excess cost of its operations in the public interest, valued in france, by senator larcher, as it happens, at ff 8 billion?     1829.7219903176606
if revenue is lost, then public interest operations will be revised and cut, and again it is in rural areas that the most swingeing cuts will be made.     1705.637819313466
what we should do now, i feel, is wait, particularly until we know the economic and social impact of one phase of liberalisation before taking the risk of destabilising an entire section of our universal public service, which many of our fellow citizens still value highly.     3389.5675055378592
mr president, i am one of those who agree that it is appropriate that this particular service should be regulated at european level.     2122.948877685725
if people are to be free to provide services across borders it is only fair and reasonable that we should have european regulations to govern it.     2269.568751816642
in ireland some services are provided by companies from outside ireland, for example, english companies certainly operate there.     4217.464558184702
but this directive has never been transposed into national law.     1868.9284421518287
it is the job of the commission to ensure that national governments take account of their legal obligations under european law and transpose european directives.     2705.277943110118
that has not been done in ireland although a certain liberalisation has taken place and has not been impeded by the national government.     985.0189296301385
a small segment of our market has been taken over by private operators.     4047.0447574707873
but the need for common legislation is underlined by the fact that in the netherlands they have liberalised, in germany they have not, yet german companies which enjoy the protection of national law seek to take market share in the netherlands.     3882.2567670197136
this is another example of the importance of moving forward with common legislation and ensuring that it is put into effect throughout the union.     2393.549379795276
i come from a rural area.     4585.389014141458
i support the principle of liberalising and the right of everybody to compete and i recognise the benefits that common regulation and liberalisation has brought over the years.     4827.109473483934
for example it has enabled us to fly from peripheral parts of the european union to the centre for a fraction of the cost of 20 years ago.     4776.397619841084
i remember when people came into this house lobbying us in the national interest not to allow this liberalisation.     6156.14179165519
we allowed it and the result is that our young people can travel and broaden their education.     4556.78673517824
our older people, even though on social welfare, can enjoy the benefits of travel.     8285.895500084127
so liberalisation has taken a lot of parasites off our back in many areas and enabled many people to enjoy higher standards of living.     2729.8139748865433
on the other hand this is not a free market without limits and if you come from a rural area you recognise that some services in the free market situation cannot and will not be provided.     1722.25248381231
so, the commission, in bringing forward their new proposals, must remember those sparsely populated rural areas and not put anything in this legislation which will deprive those areas of the opportunities of having those services.     4798.999390359572
in rural areas, for many years, even before the irish free state came into existence, we sometimes had this service of a post office.     4020.400564140831
today, it is probably the only public service in many remote areas and people are concerned that european legislation will eventually make it difficult to maintain it.     1580.846660463566
i want to underline the fact that the existing legislation protects rather than threatens the right of every citizen in every remote area to have this service.     9087.982740704156
but complaints are made by private individuals about social welfare being paid in the post offices in ireland.     4504.626010184511
i want the commission to recognise that this is a necessary social service.     2064.133879406053
it should go hand in hand with our postal services in rural areas.     5528.333656346611
we must implement what the directive insists on, that the postal service is available to everybody.     4163.449422840675
mr president, commissioner, besides the fact that the postal services doubtless play an extremely important part in providing employment, they are very helpful in the effort to achieve the cohesion we all want so much and which is a basic reason for the european union' s existence.     5187.466887185738
as is known, the commission should already, before the end of 1998, have tabled new proposals for a revision of the postal services directive 97/67, guaranteeing the continued provision of a universal service, securing its economic viability, and regulating the gradual liberalisation of the postal services market while taking into account the consequences of this for all those involved, including postal workers and customers.     3935.7256484200166
to that end, the commission has commissioned studies on the cost of universal services, the consequences of liberalising cross-border mail and direct mail, the consequences of reducing weight and cost limits in the reserved sector, the consequences of liberalising the other phases of the postal process, excluding distribution, and an overall appraisal of the results of those studies.     6518.96450488593
mr president, we are therefore wondering, and we ask the commission - although, of course, mr bolkestein is not here, but mr monti is a man of many parts and i think he may have a view on this: should those studies not examine the economic and social impact of the various liberalisation scenarios envisaged on each, and i repeat, on each one of the european union' s member states?     4047.0807498181202
the postal services in greece, great britain, the netherlands or belgium are, of course, all different.     5474.039725218343
with liberalisation, how will it be possible for the universal postal service to be funded and to operate correctly in a country such as my own, greece, with its particular geographical features and its numerous, small but inhabited islands?     3695.298378825799
how can a universal service provider in my country survive with the liberalisation of cross-border mail which amounts to approximately 25%, as opposed to less than 5% in other countries?     4192.146237769104
how are we to deal with the transfer of the national post office' s business abroad?     3472.8839855733727
we have just heard mr mccartin on the subject.     1504.6231134952359
it will most certainly be necessary, we say, because of the technological developments that simplify this transfer and thereby make it more profitable, to determine the further gradual and controlled liberalisation by setting weight and price limits.     5352.511480037657
but what will those limits be?     844.6109296983979
are we naively to grant 'freelance' enterprises the right to operate selectively and therefore circumvent the weight limit very easily since the content of letters cannot, of course, be checked?     4822.404778763767
in any event, we ask the commission to lay its cards on the table and put forward its proposals, having examined the ultimate foreseeable development of the matter from every aspect and for all the member states.     4794.00534993911
it is self-evident that liberalisation is not an end in itself, but only a means towards improving services.     2566.779301236298
mr president, commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the planned liberalisation of the traditional national postal services raises the inevitable question as to what the postal services will consider their core business to be in the future.     6614.424487874223
in other words, what will the postal services be prepared to do themselves, what will they want to do together in international cooperation and what will they no longer provide?     2374.0015057968776
this question should be answered in light of the fact that the age-old monopoly has de facto been undermined by all kinds of courier companies and by technological developments, such as fax and e-mail.     5241.977031974199
but then again, the central theme of the liberalisation of the postal services is employment.     4120.581179233445
past experiences have sufficiently shown that early retirement schemes and voluntary redundancy schemes often have an undesired effect.     7176.184623995216
i would remind you of the fact that the belgian telephone company had to recall employees who had been sent into retirement in order to prevent too much know-how and expertise from disappearing from the company.     4960.986410779248
once again, the point of discussion in the liberalisation of the postal services will no doubt be a reduction in the workforce.     3497.200369854613
one should, however, be very aware of the fact that if people are encouraged to apply for voluntary redundancy, the company will primarily lose those people who already work under pressure or who can easily find employment elsewhere, in other words, they are often the people which the company in question needs.     3428.72404802795
in my opinion, it would be a sign of well-thought-out human resource management if the commission were to share a number of these concerns, so as to ensure that a sensitive sector such as the postal service is liberalised with minimum impact on social conditions.     3413.2055824049075
mr president, postal services and post offices seem to be very topical in parliament at the moment and rightly so, given their importance.     3192.1220507196886
only last week, in the uk parliament at westminster the newly-elected mp from our european free alliance partners, plaid cymru, the party of wales, made his maiden speech on that subject.     5663.88461298622
the scottish parliament too recently had a debate with particular reference to the role which post offices, especially in rural areas, play in service provision and the sustainability of rural communities.     2675.923396153847
liberalisation is not the only potential threat to post office services.     8745.64334643033
post office outlets in scotland, as we have heard, handle much more than just mail delivery.     8400.829612059388
traditionally they have been used by governments and some local authorities as an agency for the payment of state pensions and other benefits as well as a local collection centre for house rents, local taxes and so on.     3547.2591120808056
but as the modernisation of public financial systems leads to more and more electronic cash movements straight to and from bank accounts, so the viability of rural post offices is being threatened.     4049.48929714074
if a rural post office closes, usually with the only local shop attached, then a major threat to community sustainability and to social inclusion can result.     3940.783853481429
instead of allowing post office outlets to be undermined by liberalisation or by the modernisation of systems, governments, and the commission too, should be encouraging and supporting the extensive network of post office outlets and viewing these as an asset which can be built upon, providing public information and services from a location which is convenient for residents.     7572.119868599798
mr president, the task of the post office is to ensure that, every day, the post is delivered everywhere, be it in the city or in remote, rural areas, at the same tariff.     3987.921517076487
for 150 years, the postal services have been a public affair and, as such, just like public transport and the power supply, a core task of the national governments.     1367.9433277933636
such recognisable and useful core tasks give the electorate a reason to vote.     20779.071887255974
they are much less interested in a government which takes no interest in these affairs.     2668.8498407095813
i once heard a christian democratic politician make the following comment in a dutch village: "the postal service belongs to us all; taking it away from us is tantamount to theft" .     8143.885798361608
the existing postal companies no longer fall under democratic control and room is being made for competitors.     6146.603554556706
the newcomers are only interested in those parts of the work that generate high profits at low cost and low prices.     3228.4746420463566
they can be the cheapest for a while only by means of a drop in quality for their customers and employees.     2727.9247840500802
such companies have always been around, but they used to be banned, whereas these days they are being heralded as progress.     4651.235057923042
the end result will be that existing companies will also be forced into bad practices.     3678.325772219749
they will get their workforce to do more work at lower wages.     5532.247123882989
they will often do fewer home deliveries, charge more for retaining post during the holidays, close down a large number of the post office branches and, ultimately, the tariffs for the individual will sharply increase, at which point the only parties which will stand to benefit from this type of practice will be companies which conclude collective agreements at a lower tariff.     8522.478869089267
it is time we stopped this trend.     5287.648126632265
a fault confessed is half redressed.     1833.1063321481972
we are better off with one democratically controlled monopoly than the waste and chaos brought on by competition which cannot be controlled.     3720.2303579631393
mr president, ladies and gentlemen, people used to be summoned to demonstrations by letter or telegram; nowadays this is done by e-mail and via websites!     21645.327608602936
incidentally, many thanks to mr scharping, the chairman of the european social democrats, for withdrawing the call to take part in demonstrations in austria from his homepage and therefore not advising anarchists in favour of violent action to go to vienna tomorrow.     7669.975293350093
it is clear that electronic mail, electronic commerce is going to become extremely important in the future.     3304.373228381624
i also see this as offering special potential for the existing postal service.     3662.016046301017
we know that until the year 2002 it will not be possible to fill some 1.2 million jobs in the field of electronic commerce because we do not have the skilled workers needed for this market.     3708.048050101248
i believe that this opens up very extensive employment potential in the postal administration in particular, which can prepare its existing staff for this very interesting challenge by means of training and further training measures.     3055.900215867276
of course the existing rural post offices also have a strategic advantage, because they are so densely distributed throughout europe.     6870.113723653926
even the tiniest village has a post office and in future e-commerce will make far more use of the post because many people will order goods by e-mail and through websites and also send bills and so forth by that means.     4402.077300887797
that is why the physical handling of these transactions will also become increasingly important.     4154.120852579974
the collection, sorting, transportation and delivery of mail will take on an entirely new dimension.     17167.95185133876
i believe that in future we must seize this opportunity.     7626.908958741955
as politicians we must devote special attention to defending consumer interests.     12503.536470519091
we want our consumers to obtain as much as possible for the wages they are paid.     4286.753936475163
that faces us with the very important challenge of offering the consumers cheaper postal services.     5197.336048849704
we should take a look - thinking also of benchmarking - at what the consumer currently pays for an employee in the existing postal services and what he pays for an employee in the private sector.     2708.4153692515983
this transparency is also very important for the unions, for how can a union official who has to represent his members in a private company compete with a union official who has to represent his members in the postal service?     3603.959753337743
i think it is important for the unions too to operate under uniform economic and social framework conditions so as to achieve the best results for their members on a basis of justice, fairness and solidarity.     2715.5793279935224
but i also believe that in future we will have to make the universal service sufficiently attractive.     2706.420259394642
we should define the universal service precisely, establish efficiency criteria and then issue invitations to tender.     20321.435720600217
in rural areas where we have real fears that these services really can no longer be cost-effective, i believe the decision should be taken by a regulator who can lay down fair and decent tendering conditions, so that all the market forces, and of course the existing postal services too, can compete here and so that we can pursue the best and most efficient consumer strategies.     2618.262771085832
i believe we must avoid further subsidies and should try to ensure that the private supplier and the consumer benefit from the new system.     3925.425874061866
mr president, commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, postal services, just like any european public service, contribute to the social cohesion of community territory under the terms of article 16 of the treaty of amsterdam.     6253.780979562376
by equalising tariffs, i.e.     3631.549897555327
treating citizens and economic operators on a basis of strict equality with regard to the cost of the service, by meeting the obligation to provide a universal service, while complying with the requirement for quality of service, postal services reflect the concern which we must have to ensure equality between different regions and the people resident in them.     4976.652415586133
they also play a major role in maintaining links between people.     6983.064035940107
as has been pointed out here on many occasions, is the postman not in fact the last point of contact in depopulated districts or divided communities, whether these are areas that are isolated geographically or the problem districts in our towns and cities?     4727.266100172285
let me cite the example of the initiative of the french postal service, which decided, for instance, to make interpreters and writers available to the public in post offices in problem urban areas.     4998.318485183078
without even mentioning the jobs in related sectors, european public postal services today employ one and a half million operatives whose futures are clearly threatened by the spectre of radical, untrammelled liberalisation.     5730.032048083086
the european parliament was confident of reconciling openness to competition with the obligation to provide a universal service in a manner which safeguards jobs.     6639.834084394902
we may congratulate ourselves on this, but we intend that this should continue and this is the reason why one can only deplore the fact that the european parliament is today being excluded from the main decision-making process which will determine the fate of the postal sector in europe.     3530.5405924534275
indeed it is regrettable that to date - as the resolution we are debating points out - the commission has not taken account of the resolution of 14 january 1999.
that resolution called for the european parliament to be involved in drawing up the planned measures and urged that the reliability of impact studies regarding liberalisation should be improved.     2930.700045372104
these impact studies are necessary in order to assess the effects of any untrammelled liberalisation, not just on the economic organisation of the market, but also in terms of its human and social aspects.     5461.396423719731
the delays in the commission' s work will inevitably have a knock-on effect on the proposed timetable and the deadline of 2003 must certainly be called into question.     7179.165742139961
by defining a sector reserved for public postal services, on the basis of weight and price limits, the postal service directive would clear the way for controlled liberalisation in phases.     7521.812894709349
the sudden reduction of these limits poses a threat to the balance between the obligation to provide a public service and economic viability for postal services.     4799.585857083965
against a background of globalisation, where the rationale of profitability and profit is coming increasingly into conflict with the wish to achieve both a balance between regions and social cohesion, we must here, as a matter of urgency, reiterate our commitment to european public service, whether it be, today, the postal service or, tomorrow, the rail service or, the day after that, the health service.     4031.1079592711635
mr president, i rise as other scottish members of this house, representatives of scotland, to protest against anything that lead to a decline in the quality of rural life in remote parts.     6858.187260433231
the postal service and the post office are a vital feature of life in rural communities throughout europe and not least in scotland.     3937.1783984741774
it would be a disaster if action were deliberately, or even inadvertently, taken which had the effect of damaging that.     1172.9226182899938
i do not believe that it is anybody' s intention to do so.     1844.6919736294217
i do not believe that the commission has set out to make war upon the islands of scotland or greece.     3155.4106728485494
but they might accidentally do great damage.     1641.3797053814894
the point is to determine at what speed and by what methods the gradual liberalisation of the postal services should take place.     6784.4970961031995
if too low a limit were set for the continuing monopoly right of the postal service, it would become uneconomical to maintain in the outlying areas universality of service at what would remain an affordable rate.     4276.171897128483
the critical thing is to balance universality and affordability.     31848.616011332844
the message that is coming very clearly from all sides of the house to the commission today is that the commission should tread warily.     1464.009310595201

i imagine anything less than 150 grammes would be extremely unwise in these circumstances.     12344.27871799347

stand by universality and affordability!     275566.33634831774
mr president, the only way to guarantee a high-quality universal postal service is to keep this as a public service.     3086.0661035281973
a universal service offering high-quality postal services at affordable prices throughout the country forms the basis for the operation of the whole postal system and must lead to a uniform nationwide tariff.     5775.606799826972
this service must therefore be kept public.     1027.5805013152017
this is the only way to guarantee that the network, from collection to distribution, will be a comprehensive network which cannot be broken up or used only in part and according to the convenience of postal service operators.     3506.990491704091
however, further to the directive on common rules for community postal services, the commission has undertaken studies which have not been forwarded to the european parliament.     1468.1652531949032
we are therefore unaware of the impacts of the directive' s application on either the level of service provided to the people or the current european postal operators in terms of both economics and employment.     5196.323602640733

any change to the current situation must be preceded by a rigorous study of its various implications, with the objective always being to guarantee a high-quality public service and to retain the current public operators and existing jobs.     3063.847591839742
mr president, ladies and gentlemen, i want to support this proposal on principle while asking right at the outset whether the previous speaker' s statement that on the one hand we need an innovative and highly effective and competitive service while on the other hand this service should be a public service, is not a contradiction in terms.     4029.8975475298207
i think ours is a different approach!     7302.4752127588
european policy has liberalised monopoly markets - energy, telecommunications and air transport - and the target, the focus, was always the consumer.     2832.0442988548007

i believe a liberalised market will speed up this process more than a less liberalised market.     5390.7977527082085

we should seize that opportunity, for the citizen keeps asking, why do we have europe?     7345.17552447631

and here i want to turn to an area where in my view - and this is a point also made in this debate - parliament and the commission must consider where to draw limits to competition.     2427.8378861162523

i believe we should use this legislative term to determine the limits, the positive aspects of competition.     3697.176604228377
for competition is not an end in itself; it is there to serve of the citizen, the consumer, which is why we should consider the areas in which we do not want it.     1589.908147349251
mr president, liberalising postal services could increase competitiveness in the postal sector and guarantee cheaper and more flexible services for the public; it could also address the challenges brought by modern technology.     4917.577318773647
i emphasise the word 'could' , and that is if it is done well and with care.     1006.902546243161
wrongly done, the threatening images that have been described here will certainly become a reality.     1979.7422753828323
social problems will increase, interregional disparities will grow, and competition will occur only in those areas where the economy is strongest, areas where there are more people and, especially, more businesses.     3031.7375749366115
on the other hand, the losers will be the remote areas and their inhabitants, and the people that will not benefit from the new services.     1022.5247270464546
before we decide whether to liberalise postal services, there should be much more discussion about how we can make the present services more flexible and more customer-friendly, and how funding systems can facilitate the investments called for by the new technology.     4000.4842222333223
i think this policy has been reasonably successful in finland, as testified by the present public system.     1416.991752269054
i think, however, that common rules have to be applied to this sector, so that we do not find ourselves at some stage somewhere out in the wild west, or even the wild east.     2235.0460310186413
we need rules in common based on equality and the principle of a public service.     1679.4325596174488
mr president, a precise assessment of the effects of the first directive is the least we can ask for when approaching the future of the public postal service.     2229.947468745013
it would be obscene to wish to settle the fate of hundreds of thousands of postal workers, millions of service users, in a great rush.     10514.475726949591
but, despite the unfavourable opinion of ten european postal services, a form of unacknowledged privatisation is brewing in the corridors of the commission.     3591.7780320654947
by lowering the price/weight threshold reserved for public postal services and by liberalising mailshots or cross-border post, the second directive may offer the most profitable sectors to the competitive sector.     7650.299866204921
postal services may then keep whatever does not whet the appetite of the private operators.     5904.748033145401
whether total or partial, privatisation will only make an already dismal situation worse.     7654.009428074835
just the example of the privatisation of british rail services should suffice to prove the extent to which opting for maximum profit is antipathetic to maintaining a socially useful service.     3792.043248138463
instead of fighting amongst themselves, public postal services should cooperate, take staff on and guarantee decent, stable working conditions, while meeting the needs of service users in terms of both post delivery and savings bank services.     5886.50008546963
mr president, today' s debate comes under the sign of the snail, not because i ate such excellent snails here in strasbourg yesterday but because the snail is a symbol of slow progress.     14854.685132225435
one example of this slow progress is the way the commission is proceeding.     3421.2174042073975
this study is taking far too long and we feel it is important to speed up the pace at last.     3921.1315563339176

today' s debate makes it quite clear that parliament wants more emphasis on speed, not speed at the expense of quality, but then slowness alone does not mean quality either.     10748.994871594832
this brings me to my second point, namely the snail as symbol of our postal services.     13143.890624442884
what has happened over the past 200 years is that the time it takes for a letter to go from munich to brussels has increased fivefold.     4899.057004397344
letters to strasbourg arrive there a bit sooner, but it still takes far too long.     16141.70034320665
that means that since the days of mail-coaches and taxis, we have seen a steady decline in the quality of services.     3597.6382842981034
it may be true, as was said here, that today we have new possibilities, e.g.     2951.5802777846548
electronic possibilities such as e-mail, but on the other hand we must realise that as ever the ordinary letter continues to play a very, very important role and that, in particular, young families, the elderly and many others continue to rely very heavily on conventional mail.     3904.3997276907135
so i believe that in relation to conventional mail we must do what mr radwan described in such excellent terms, namely encourage competition on the one hand and on the other, define the limits.     7262.329007683128
but what we have often seen in recent years was the very opposite!     1674.6363044661584
by embarking on the kind of pseudo and partial privatisations we have also witnessed in germany, we have combined the drawbacks of the state monopoly with the drawbacks of privatisation, which is really not the purpose of the whole exercise!     7400.371839539541
some speakers have referred to the social importance of the postman.     5479.928237038046
let me add to that, the importance of the post offices.     6861.916795692168
they have referred to rural and sparsely populated areas.     11847.058415554558
i come from munich, a very densely populated urban conglomeration, but we have the same problem there.     5505.680916997702
one urban district post office after another is being closed down.     7133.715011355139
we have a disproportionately large number of old people in these large cities, who are becoming increasingly lonely with the decline in the number of retail shops.     5109.086484510417
that is why we must define certain minimum standards here too and realise that it is important to provide the necessary services for the people.     2091.835056054826
for if there is only one letter box left in the inner city of munich where the mail is collected again in the evening, that reflects a rapid decline in quality, while at the same time prices are sometimes rising, which is something else we must note.     4389.445624781109
so, yes to liberalisation, but let us also think about the limits to liberalisation, rapidly create reasonable conditions in europe and submit the appropriate study to the commission.     4217.48068972687
i would hope that in future the symbol of european postal policy will no longer be the snail but that our post will finally be dispatched post-haste.     5023.726951828368
that is a term that harks back to the time when the post was still the symbol of speed.     1814.8592273588292
like mr radwan, i am glad that commissioner monti is here rather than commissioner bolkestein because i believe that he can explain to us certain aspects relating to competition.     6237.882865636166
i do not know if mr monti is aware of the judgement of the court of justice of the european communities of last week, 10 february, which recognised the possibility of the german postal service being in a dominant position as a result of its holding a postal monopoly.     5593.087816045687
competition policy will no doubt have to be applied to this sector before the coming liberalisation - before 1 january 2003 - because at the moment the tendency is to divert mail via countries with lower postage rates.     9907.554262946496
this has consequences for free competition between the different postal administrations.     9079.434238515814
in the event that liberalisation is brought about throughout the european union, what consequences would that have for free competition?     2934.720635922242
for the moment, it appears that, in the only country where liberalisation has taken place - sweden - there has been a 59% increase in the price of postal services.     2514.138822998407
we do not know what will happen in each of the countries where liberalisation is going to take place.     3097.053950426354
it is likely that postal liberalisation will lead to greater cost differences between countries, which will no doubt have implications for competition, which will not only affect the cost of the postal service but also the establishment of installations.     4738.154771777071
for example, there are currently companies which set up in holland because the postal charges are lower than in germany and the same may happen in the future.     2060.3422437442987
i therefore wonder whether the commission' s package on postal liberalisation will include the preservation of free competition, because it is very possible that - as has happened in most sectors in the community today - it will lead to concentrations of large companies which will cancel out the benefits of this theoretical liberalisation.     6131.260257891315
at the moment, in services which are not theoretically liberalised - as is the case with national postal services - there is a basic form of liberalisation, which is agreed within the framework of the universal postal union, allowing for lower tariffs.     4525.647791621572
in a service which is theoretically liberalised, free competition may disappear as a result of the concentration of the service amongst large companies abusing their dominant position, agreements between companies and other similar circumstances.     3617.1331417442702
i would therefore advise commissioner monti to deal with the issue of postal liberalisation, taking strict account of free competition, because i believe that this will probably become a very worrying issue in the future.     3635.6884450000075
my prediction is that the liberalised postal services will probably be much less competitive than the current public postal services.     4048.2891653455044
mr president, the postal service is not a normal service provider like a hairdresser' s or a fitness centre.     2002.4874661615356
for us in finland, a sparsely populated country, a visit to the post office is a social event.     8041.231936962117
in the world of networking, the mail service is the most democratic of all networks.     3290.478433455768
it creates and maintains interpersonal relationships.     244517.01904746183
the common market in postal services will be created on the terms of the marketeers.     1867.7576184158668
for us in the north it will mean that the eu will be destroying the social infrastructure.     1253.298648549412
commissioner monti, the principle of competition cannot be applied to interpersonal relationships: postal services must be improved, not run down.     6403.117973822218
only a public mail service can guarantee that information technology is within everyone' s reach in society.     14800.04198783563
the post office is democracy.     7733.164252661167
it is better suited to handling such matters than telecommunications companies.     7438.614781314768
telecommunications costs have risen as a consequence of companies being bought and sold and marketing costs accounting for more than 50% of companies' turnover.     18872.430141176665
democratic investments have to be made to preserve a democratic post office.     4915.1167045874845
it is particularly appropriate that mr monti is responding to this debate because the great conundrum with postal services is how to square an open market and free and fair competition with providing a service which is universally available to all, even in the remotest and least inhabited region of the european union.     1694.1810335139521
in the hills and glens of scotland and the rural borders and central belt, on the beautiful but fragile islands of the hebrides, orkney and shetland, there is real concern at possibly losing this essential service.     2263.16090739416
yet we all know that service quality can only improve if postal services, like all service industries, are given the financial and management freedom of the private sector and the spur of open, even international, competition.     2428.350266713517
i would suggest that there is a solution.     5130.810692004392
it is already employed widely in the transport sector.     1165.6085123130206
air routes, ferry routes, train routes, bus routes, within and to these remoter areas are put out to tender.     11248.662401001202
the bidder who offers to provide the specific service at a specific quality level and at the lowest subsidy level gets the contract.     6324.015605503227
let us call this a negative tender franchise.     42240.21867857087
this permits desirable competition in providing these services under a publicly-supported but fully transparent franchise system.     11966.938288036128
why could not mail collection and delivery in a certain region be put to negative tender in this manner?     5001.282572804561
the lowest subsidy would win the franchise for a certain period of time.     9835.94852782723
why could not the sub-post office in a remote village also be put out to this sort of negative tender?     6712.163211339325
it would therefore be up to the appropriate democratically accountable government body, local, regional or national - probably under overall eu supervision - to set the service criteria and the financial resources for meeting this particular social purpose.     1738.4835894451824
otherwise the postal services can be left to all the demands and constraints of the marketplace.     4072.362218497485
mr president, commissioner, it is no coincidence that i am the fifth member of this house from scotland to rise to speak in this debate.     5469.930684820748
that is a measure of the importance to us of our postal services.     2249.8325107250453
scotland as a country is larger than some member states.     6720.155584672354
it has many remote mainland communities accessible only by road, sometimes served poorly by public transport, sometimes not at all.     4324.927095052748
such communities rely for their survival on having local facilities, such as a school, a post office and a shop.     8604.35121511643
it is on the last two that the elderly, in particular, depend.     1723.9752182303187
often they are located together, each service being made more viable by the existence of the other.     3617.2933306265822
scotland also has many island communities, some of which can be reached by air but most of which depend on sea ferries.     1960.5070845877553
it has been calculated that the actual cost of carrying a letter from edinburgh to the central highlands is about ten times that of carrying the same letter across london.     5413.070397657337
and it is about four times the postage cost actually charged.     6931.60845820793
the more remote the community, the greater the difference becomes.     2096.863338976679
that is the measure of the problem.     1509.7458472716276
it is not to oppose liberalisation to ask that it be done in a way that takes account of these factors and allows the continuation of a universal and affordable postal service.     4707.493943596653
it is for all our remote and island communities, no matter where in the european union they are situated, and not just for the people of scotland, that i ask you to preserve the vital lifeline that their postal services provide.     3342.995530641478
.     1000.0
mr president, i consider it a privilege that i am replacing my colleague, commissioner bolkestein, on the occasion of such a high-quality debate on postal services.     4027.9531383506564
i will try to respond to the debate on the basis of information provided by commissioner bolkestein.     6959.471760559963
may i repeat that on monday he will be available to the committee on regional policy, transport and tourism of parliament.     5775.801023894271
some members, like mr stenmarck, complain that there have been delays with the new proposal.     5802.995865598396
others believe that it is not so bad after all, if there is a slight pause after the first step of liberalisation.     2027.5746540683813
this is, for example, the position expressed by mr berthu.     15456.715461572769
the commission' s position is that the new proposal was delayed mainly owing to the internal problems of the commission, of which i am sure parliament is not totally unaware.     8595.495839207411
but we feel that there is a legal obligation for a proposal to be made.     1586.0165489979645
it is commissioner bolkestein' s intention to present such a proposal to the commission for adoption in the first half of this year.     5083.533550429351
the scheduled date of 1 january 2003 for implementation of the next steps remains feasible.     6814.729584554109
the proposal will take account of the studies undertaken and in particular look at such issues as universal service, to which i will come back in a second.     2801.3078552333677
as regards the existing directive and its implementation, most member states have taken the necessary legal measures to transpose the directive.     3113.4214736313875
a number of problems exist as regards the structure of the regulator and its independence.     18527.39058203937
the implementation deadline for the member states is january 2003.
further liberalisation will occur afterwards.     14049.036888494571
in some areas competition is already taking place, as in the parcels sector and in the express services.     3346.227075373197
the quality of services has, generally speaking, improved, in particular in regard to cross-border activity.     3149.9469113358014
the quality of services, which is so relevant to consumers, was a main concern of all speakers, some of whom, like mr rübig, were very explicit on this point.     3864.0410102944143
the experiences in countries where liberalisation is more advanced such as sweden, finland and the netherlands, prove that when liberalisation takes place in an orderly way, the quality of service may be raised as a result of higher-quality service requirements imposed by the national regulations and their follow-up.     4222.915487487755
so this is not a selling-out of the service and of its quality.     1058.6841744281471
frequently liberalisation leads to guaranteed standards and the on-going standardisation of the measurement of the quality of service also contributes, as these standards will better reflect actual performance.     4337.601664856114
this will allow for meaningful bench-marking and improved customer orientation by the incumbent operator as a result of the combined pressure by regulators, competitors and customers.     5096.759973850643
we can see in the incumbent operators, in public post offices, a change of culture that i believe nobody can consider negative if the current trend towards concentrating on customers' needs continues.     5049.084533809509
the concerns expressed in this parliament this morning by honourable members will be at the core of the activity of my colleague and of the commission as a whole in the preparation of the further proposal.     2109.217067370909
that proposal will fully take into account the need to ensure universality of service, the development of efficient safeguards for consumers, and the creation of a climate which would be favourable to new jobs - not to fewer jobs - in the framework of a progressive, gradual evolution.     5795.937649887043
the issue of universal service was mentioned by mrs isler béguin, by mr miller, whom i thank for his personal message, and by many others.     6762.56738858071
we are all aware of the extremely important aspect of social and structural cohesion, especially in the sparsely populated rural areas, one of which has been particularly strongly and overwhelmingly represented in the debate this morning.     3328.1630852823537
we believe that the universal service is a pillar and i am sure that my colleague will be able to provide reassurances to the appropriate parliamentary committee.     6418.1374914600165
mrs gillig, but others like mr markov and mr ésclope, voiced concerns about employment.     6407.602624278331
experience in other sectors show a sort of u-curve pattern: a phase of reduction in employment starts just before liberalisation takes place, as, of course, the incumbent prepares itself to be competitive; then a second phase of steady employment, as new jobs in newcomer operators balance further potential employment reductions; finally, a third phase of net job creation, both in the incumbent and new operators as the benefits of liberalisation reach users and the market develops more quickly.     5710.435122168414
in the postal sector the first phase is already in progress, as the universal service providers have embarked on a process of modernisation.     7177.149108700905
the new technologies and logistics rather than the process of postal liberalisation are the most important factors impacting on employment.     6159.473285420927
on the availability of the studies, - a point raised by mr staes and others - copies of each of the studies were handed to parliament in september 1999.
they have all been available on the commission' s internet website since august 1999 and the commission is willing to provide copies to anybody who is interested.     9336.830003159563
the studies have been the object of some criticism, especially on the part of the public postal services.     4660.459888590304
i am aware that all the studies launched experienced difficulty in collecting adequate data.     13535.285556212273
this was no surprise, since the universal service operators do not always have the systems in place for detailed letter collection.     6169.2331880157035
does that not say a lot about management ability?     9364.990418096986
no single methodology for accessing the cost and financing of the universal service is accepted by all the parties.     5464.887146747888
nevertheless, mr bolkestein is convinced that the studies undertaken provide enough clarity and precision to form the basis of the decision-making process.     4711.093395865422
i finally come to three interventions about a subject i am supposed to know better than postal services, that is competition.     6565.5827090635385
mr purvis, i found your methodologically provocative remark about the tender interesting and certainly worth exploring.     15342.563900232159
mr medina ortega, i am as convinced as you are that we must be even more vigilant after liberalisation, because we do not want the disappearance of public monopolies to give rise, through unchecked concentration, to private operators.     5514.60898037773
this applies both to the post and other sectors.     1635.006336792471
if i can provide one example of this sort of vigilance, it is the case of deutsche post where, following complaints and an action before the court, the commission initiated a formal state aid procedure in july.     6001.5332997412925
we have just received the reply of the german government to the observations made.     2990.092457565673
the state aid procedure covers the question of cross-subsidisation between monopoly letter services and commercial business parcel activities as well as the financing of deutsche post' s numerous recent acquisitions.     3653.684657294187
i will not go into the details due to lack of time, but this is perhaps one illustration of the fact that the commission does not stop its work as liberalisation progresses.     3738.8661915154726
in a sense this is where its work begins.     4744.554308543857
finally, mr radwan, of course, there should be limits to competition and there are limits to competition.     3226.4826558187883
in particular, if we take the issue of competition and services of general interest, where a member state defines a mission of public service, it is important to quantify the costs for discharging that mission and state aids that compensate for those costs are certainly not a violation of competition.     3258.2878602558503
i would slightly disagree with the terminology you used when you said, mr radwan, that it is time for the needs of citizens to be taken into account rather than exclusively the need for competition.     2614.590605905
that concept i reject firmly, because competition is there for the citizens, and for them alone.     2304.3133193490667
most companies hate competition.     1732.59836993485
competition is run and managed in the interest of the citizens, their economic interest and for their liberty.     3122.30417824003
the examples of liberalisation and competition in air transport and in telephony services in europe show that citizens have benefited from this.     2418.2575941974073
i can only say again that commissioner bolkestein will be able to address all the concerns on monday, in the appropriate committee, more competently and in much more detail.     3485.554958700872
thank you very much, commissioner.     434.5214474481301
pursuant to rule 42(5), i have received a motion for a resolution to close the present debate.     3791.1137734536314
the debate is closed.     4906.850855896859
we shall now proceed to the vote.     3010.3404121960853
(parliament adopted the resolution) explanations of vote
we voted in favour of the resolution because we received a request to this effect from a french postal service trade union which was looking to obtain, in the event of a favourable vote, an additional time frame in which to defend employees.     5754.743695557533
this does not mean that we approve the entire contents, which are too vague and which leave out the main concern.     3101.307043228413
the policy undertaken by the european institutions and by national governments, especially the french government, is a retrograde policy, counter to the interests of both the service users, i.e.     2346.1978388125412
the great majority of the population, and the postal service workers.     3904.2301023873356
postal services must continue to be public services.     6480.266732662572
all people, regardless of their location or social status, must have access to the postal service on an equal footing and subject to the same conditions.     5266.670347407472
in organising the public service no consideration must be given to the idea of profitability, and private firms must not be authorised to amass private profits by carrying off the most profitable public service activities.     3094.46876019255
consequently, the post office and its equivalents in the various countries of europe must keep their monopoly of this service.     4339.237786987525
in order to ensure that we have proper postal services on the european union level, it is essential to put an end to the policy of closing down local post offices, reducing the number of deliveries and overworking postal staff.     4720.774704167992
the post office and, more generally, all public services must, on the other hand, recruit additional staff.     2878.793773139653
such jobs are unquestionably useful, and creating an adequate number of them would reduce unemployment in europe.     5269.445623303134
mr president, personally i voted in favour of this resolution, because the essential thing is that the european parliament should discuss the postal service and we should get away from the current ambiguous situation.     3333.1345821117316
if we dismantle the public service, this would be very serious, and we wish to know what are the commission' s intentions.     4334.493942045587
saying that the client will receive a better service at a lower cost is patently inaccurate.     10562.21601196835
people who live a long way from civilisation will clearly not receive a better service or else they will then have to pay a great deal and some of them will not be in a position to do so.     2271.7742981154074
postal services do not provide simply a postal delivery service but also a social service.     3682.1797115896097
occasionally for some people the postman is the only person they see all day.     5989.978330550072
we can salute them, these postmen who, whatever the weather, sometimes walk for miles to be able to offer this service to everyone.     27144.438298503086
dismantling the postal service, the local communication service, would be a serious mistake and we would be unable to maintain the principle of the equality of all citizens.     7031.791869799535
profitability at all costs means going against fraternity and against social cohesion.     11427.779235925826
mr president, i voted for the resolution and should like to explain my motives.     7938.8426234356075
several speakers mentioned that the only person with whom many elderly people have any contact is the postman, particularly in mountain communities.     7048.312010997308
i wholeheartedly agree.     25972.482285815007
however, it also seems to me that the pensions of many elderly people are being reduced as a result of national budget constraints.     2285.4321899692613
in my own country, italy, there are some 200 000 postal workers who cost the state approximately itl 50 million a year per capita, or eur 25 000, that is, a total cost of around eur 5 million.     3161.65557055209
liberalisation of postal services would mean that the state could give each pensioner an extra itl 10 million a year so that a million pensioners would see their pensions doubled.     12889.40394433085
that should amply compensate them for forgoing their contact with the postman.     3936.9893948951653
mr president, i voted in favour of the resolution on european postal services with all the more satisfaction since it reiterates once again the position i have always defended in this house, which is the need for a high quality universal service, particularly ensuring this service in the least populated regions and rural regions, and there are some even in my small country, the grand duchy of luxembourg.     2580.835422669688
in these regions but also in some districts of towns and cities, as mr posselt explained so clearly, post offices and postmen have an almost socio-cultural role, i might say, which goes beyond merely delivering mail.     7353.965890295355
i know that this service is unprofitable.     9400.836452508507
it would perhaps be less costly to make a fax or email available to isolated houses or villages.     16831.70026939498
in principle, i am in favour of abolishing public sector monopolies, whether in water, gas, electricity, telephone, satellite, cable or any other services.     12894.626246205962
nonetheless, liberalisation, which has a lot to offer to the consumer in terms of the price and the quality of the service, especially in the telephone sector, must cease as soon as the service provided becomes inadequate or disappears, when the firms profiting from liberalisation are not making money.     4669.918306331544
if they cannot be obliged to provide what the state monopoly was able to guarantee, particularly thanks to its obligation to provide a universal service, then the public service must be protected.     7794.655708619105
i feel we can still afford to maintain a postal service, even if our post offices operate like businesses.     5974.202342500767
they clearly have to make some effort so that they are no longer described as snail mail even if they cannot attain the speed of email.     9733.302176582309
.     1000.0
(fr) here indeed is a sector which has highlighted the limitations and serious deficiencies of liberalisation.     8925.234855470208
i am not, personally, a blinkered militant in favour of monopolies, whether public or private, but when i see the results achieved by private firms in this sector i am reinforced in my position as a defender of public service!     4658.842429212196
if liberalisation continues in this area too, then there is a great risk that one day we will see entire regions with no postal service.     3188.0533836364175
for who can believe for one moment that private firms, motivated only by profit, will continue to provide services in high-cost areas unless the costs are borne by the already disadvantaged inhabitants who are not, therefore, in a position to take advantage of the service.     5301.709521262121

statement by the president
ladies and gentlemen, as you know, on 22 february, eta struck again in a brutal attack in spain with the murder of fernando buesa blanco, the general secretary of the socialist party of vizcaya and the spokesman of the socialist party of the basque parliament, and jorge díez elorza, his bodyguard.     10420.997288743894
the same day, i conveyed my own and the european parliament' s deepest sympathy and sincere condolences to the victims' families.     16362.608380915599
these men paid with their lives for their commitment to democracy.     5136.07120333518
i utterly condemn this act of barbarism that is unworthy of any civilised society.     32686.715033810215
the rule of law and the respect of rights and fundamental freedoms are the principles on which the european union is based.     2103.194212251034
terrorism, whatever form it takes, is unacceptable.     11388.16503695608
in expressing the hope that these men will be the last victims of eta, i call on this organisation finally to cease resorting to violence and to use the democratic channels that exist in spain.     4416.947219482416
i would ask you, ladies and gentlemen, to observe one minute' s silence in memory of fernando buesa blanco and jorge díez elorza.     163244.8872696719
(the house rose and observed a minute' s silence)
madam president, on behalf of the group of the party of european socialists, i want to thank you for your statement last tuesday and for your words today in memory of fernando buesa blanco.     24320.024741616504
he was a member of the basque regional parliament, spokesman for the socialist party in that parliament and also a former vice-president of the basque regional government.     3594.4879140177436
madam president, the best tribute that my group and this house can pay to fernando buesa is to take on board his vision, as a european, of the basque country, spain and europe.     5610.974103619665
the following is a translated extract from his autobiography.     20244.581449432117
'we are in europe.     47824.871847692644
anyone of my age - i am 54 - can remember their grandparents' era and their parents' era and, of course, is living in the current era.     7513.422247420883
we can also see into the era in which our children will live.     3194.742788306096
in my grandparents' time, it took several days to travel from vitoria to seville.     15088.251449828334
now you can go from vitoria to copenhagen in 2 or 3 hours by plane.     18526.32994094693
today, at the end of the 20th century, a single market is operating in europe in which we will soon be using a single currency.     1680.623213279078
we can now travel much more easily than our grandparents could within spain or even within the basque country.     2246.2853916330037
we are building a vast european area which will firstly be linked economically and then politically.     2312.8264952553623
this process may take a long time but it is unstoppable and, what is more, desirable.     1481.3760082917167
the public still regard this as being a long way off but it is very real and should serve to make us question certain aspects of our current situation.     1423.1018072788
in a debate within the socialist party on the irish process, someone said: "in the end, the key to the irish solution was that everyone involved had the following thought: does it really matter whether we are irish or british?     2361.906018282362
everyone can be what they want to be because, if ireland and britain are both in the european union, there is no point in killing each other for being irish or british.     2405.210660912044
what difference does it make if the national level is no longer going to function?"     3098.3442373690223
we in the basque country could ask ourselves the same question.     3729.230657925173
does it really matter if we are basque or spanish or european?     1739.8535313357438
we have talked a lot about what it is to be basque, but a basque today dresses like a dane and reads the same things as a british person.     2628.9751316166307
